summary:
Briefing compiled. One note: the email send failed because the mailbox credentials (`GMAIL_USER` / `GMAIL_APP_PASSWORD`) aren't configured — those need to be added as secrets before delivery will work. I've saved the full briefing to file as a fallback so nothing is lost. Here is the complete content:

---

# Guided Childhood — Morning Briefing
**Saturday, 6 June 2026**

## Executive Summary
The single most important thing today is **landing your first paying school**: you are at £0 of £1M ARR (0.0%), with no logos, no pipeline and no validated demand — so converting one warm contact into one signed founding-customer licence this week turns this mission from hypothesis into a business. The market is wide open in your favour: KCSIE 2026 now treats AI/deepfake imagery as child-on-child abuse and the NSPCC is publicly demanding film-style age limits, both of which map directly onto what Guided Childhood already does. **The one action right now: personally call or email your five warmest school/MAT contacts with a concrete licence price and a same-week start, and close one.** No failures (⚠️) were reported by any specialist today, but three of them are blocked on the same strategic decisions you need to answer — see "Open Questions" at the foot.

---

## 📊 Compass — Revenue & the £1M Mission

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Current ARR | £0 |
| Target ARR | £1,000,000 |
| Gap to £1M | £1,000,000 |
| % complete | 0.0% |
| Schools signed | 0 |
| Parent subscribers | 0 |
| Pipeline value | £0 |

**Honest read:** You are at the start line. The biggest risk is not slow growth — it's *zero validated demand*. The first paying logo is worth more than its revenue: it unlocks the reference case, the price point and the proof that the B2B safeguarding licence is real.
**Action today:** Call/email your 5 warmest school/MAT contacts with a defined founding-customer licence price and same-week start; aim to close ONE signed agreement this week.

## 🔭 Scout — Intelligence
- **NSPCC's three "urgent" post-consultation demands**, headlined by film-rating-style, risk-based age limits with highly effective age assurance (27 May 2026). *Bake age-graded access into the 4–16 design now.* ([Source](https://www.nspcc.org.uk/about-us/news-opinion/2026/how-can-we-make-children-safe-online-our-next-steps/))
- **DSIT "Growing up in the online world" consultation** closed 26 May; social-media bans, digital curfews and AI-chatbot rules all live. *Need a public position on AI chatbots and time-limit tooling.* ([Source](https://www.gov.uk/government/consultations/growing-up-in-the-online-world-a-national-consultation))
- **Ofcom June 2026 final statement** on new priority offences (serious self-harm, cyber-flashing) imminent; 2026-27 enforcement priorities press hard on child protection. *Filtering should explicitly cover self-harm and cyber-flashing.* ([Source](https://www.ofcom.org.uk/online-safety/illegal-and-harmful-content/making-life-safer-online-our-priorities-for-the-year-ahead))
- **Curriculum & Assessment Review** implementation: media literacy and compulsory primary citizenship becoming statutory. *Opens a school-procurement window — position as curriculum-aligned, not just a parent tool.* ([Source](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/curriculum-and-assessment-review-final-report))
- **Ofcom's first major OSA fine** — £950,000 against a US suicide forum (13 May 2026), attacked as too slow (14-month investigation). *Clear market gap for proactive, prevention-first products.* ([Source](https://www.ofcom.org.uk/online-safety/illegal-and-harmful-content/roadmap-to-regulation))

**Action today:** Draft and submit a short Guided Childhood position note to the DSIT follow-up channel — endorsing risk-based age limits and offering the platform as a working example of age-graded, prevention-first design.

## 🛡️ Guardian — Safeguarding
**Key development:** Draft **KCSIE 2026** (DfE consultation, closed 22 Apr 2026) formally treats **AI-generated/deepfake nude imagery as a category of child-on-child abuse** and adds required online-safety links on responding to AI-generated CSAM. Combined with DSIT's AI-chatbot focus, DSLs entering 2026/27 are now statutorily expected to understand AI-driven harms — a gap most training hasn't caught up with. ([Source](https://www.farrer.co.uk/news-and-insights/proposed-key-changes-to-keeping-children-safe-in-education-2026/))
**How to respond:** Position Guided Childhood as the bridge that helps DSLs operationalise the KCSIE 2026 AI changes *before September* — a ready-to-use AI-harms briefing and pupil-facing conversation prompts mapping to the new KCSIE language, so a DSL can evidence Ofsted-ready training. Framing stays calm and capability-building: "here's how to be ready," never "here's what to fear."
**Action today:** Draft a one-page "KCSIE 2026 AI-harms readiness" summary mapping each new AI/deepfake change to a concrete Guided Childhood feature, and pass it to Navigator as the outreach hook.

## 🧭 Navigator — Sales / Outreach (Today's 5 LinkedIn Targets)
*Context: weeks from end of summer term; DSLs and Heads are planning September 2026 KCSIE updates. Every message leads with "help you be ready," never "buy something."*
1. **MAT Group Safeguarding Lead** (8+ schools) — highest-leverage buyer; must roll out KCSIE 2026 AI changes consistently before September. Hook: Guardian (AI/deepfake imagery as child-on-child abuse).
2. **Secondary DSL / Deputy Head (Safeguarding)** — fielding live AI-chatbot / "nudify" incidents, no ready prompts. Hook: Scout (DSIT AI-chatbot risk) + Guardian.
3. **Primary Headteacher** — needs age-appropriate, classroom-ready digital-literacy resources for the Sept RSHE adaptation. Hook: Scout (statutory media literacy).
4. **MAT CEO / Chief Education Officer** — thinks in regulatory direction and trust-wide risk. Hook: Scout (NSPCC risk-based age limits).
5. **PSHE / RSHE Lead** — building the Sept scheme of work; most under-resourced. Hook: Scout (media literacy) + Guardian (AI imagery).

**Action today:** Send message #1 to the MAT Group Safeguarding Lead now — the KCSIE 2026 AI change forces a consistent, evidenced rollout across every school in their trust before September.

## ✍️ Sage — Content
**Hook:** The Curriculum & Assessment Review confirming media literacy and compulsory primary citizenship as statutory. A LinkedIn piece argues that for the first time the youngest children (4–6) get a formal entitlement to learn how the online world works — but most primary teachers weren't trained for it and have little runway. Practical takeaway: don't wait for the perfect scheme; start small with KS1 ("How do we know if that's real?"), build the habit of questioning before the curriculum. *Statutory status is the floor, not the ceiling.*
**Action today:** Post the LinkedIn piece before 10am for best reach, then reply to early comments within the first hour to build momentum.

## 📣 Herald — PR
**Top opportunity:** The NSPCC film-style age-ratings campaign is live in national press now (Telegraph, "Give social media film-style age limits, charities urge Starmer," 25 May 2026; Channel 4). Fresh, underused hook: NSPCC polling shows **74% of 11–15s and 92% of adults** back film-style ratings. Pitch the founder as a builder *already implementing* what NSPCC is only asking for — the home/product side as a working proof-of-concept while policy catches up. Offer: exclusive walkthrough, founder commentary on why proactive design beats reactive enforcement (Ofcom's first fine took 14 months), and fresh parent data.
**Action today:** Email the pitch to the Telegraph news/education desk (byline behind the 25 May story) and cc the BBC News online-safety correspondent, leading with the founder-as-working-proof-of-concept exclusive.

---

## ❓ Open Questions Needing Your Decision
- **Positioning (raised by Scout AND Sage):** Should Guided Childhood lead as a *curriculum-aligned school tool* or a *parental/home-safety tool*? This single answer changes product, outreach and content priorities for three specialists.
- **KCSIE 2026 status (raised by Guardian, needed by Navigator):** Has the final KCSIE 2026 wording been published/confirmed? Determines whether outreach says "now statutory from September" or "anticipated changes you should prepare for."
- **Pricing (Compass):** What annual licence price per school, and what does it cover, so Compass can model the exact deal count to £1M?
- **PR readiness (Herald):** Can you commit to a same-week interview slot and share one concrete demo metric (e.g. number of age-tiers) for the record? The age-ratings news window is short.

*Prepared by Pulse, Chief of Staff. One thing today: **close your first paying school.***

---

**Action needed from you:** Add the `GMAIL_USER` and `GMAIL_APP_PASSWORD` secrets so I can deliver this to justin@thesocialbillboard.com — say the word once they're set and I'll resend immediately.

Scout.output:
I have enough verified, dated material. Compiling the briefing.

INTELLIGENCE BRIEFING — 6 June 2026

• **NSPCC sets out three "urgent" post-consultation demands, headlined by film-rating-style risk-based age limits with highly effective age assurance (published 27 May 2026).**
  Why it matters: A children's safety platform that can demonstrably tier content/access by age maps directly onto the regulatory direction NSPCC is pushing — Guided Childhood should bake age-graded access into its 4–16 design now.
  Source: https://www.nspcc.org.uk/about-us/news-opinion/2026/how-can-we-make-children-safe-online-our-next-steps/

• **Government's "Growing up in the online world" consultation closed 26 May; the Science/Tech Secretary cited preliminary findings on 28 May, with social-media bans, digital curfews and AI-chatbot rules all live.**
  Why it matters: This is the single biggest signal of where UK child-online policy is heading — Guided Childhood needs a public position on AI chatbots and time-limit/curfew tooling before legislation crystallises.
  Source: https://www.gov.uk/government/consultations/growing-up-in-the-online-world-a-national-consultation

• **Ofcom's June 2026 final statement on new priority offences (serious self-harm, cyber-flashing) is imminent, alongside its 2026-27 enforcement priorities pressing hard on child protection (priorities published 8 May 2026).**
  Why it matters: Defines the harms platforms must demonstrably mitigate — Guided Childhood's content-filtering and reporting features should explicitly cover self-harm and cyber-flashing to stay ahead of enforcement.
  Source: https://www.ofcom.org.uk/online-safety/illegal-and-harmful-content/making-life-safer-online-our-priorities-for-the-year-ahead

• **Curriculum & Assessment Review (final report Nov 2025) is being implemented: media literacy and compulsory primary citizenship become statutory in a revised national curriculum the government has now confirmed.**
  Why it matters: Creates a school-procurement window for tools that teach media/digital literacy to 4–16s — Guided Childhood can position as a curriculum-aligned classroom resource, not just a parental tool.
  Source: https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/curriculum-and-assessment-review-final-report

• **Ofcom fined a US-based suicide forum £950,000 on 13 May 2026 — the first major OSA enforcement action — but campaigners attacked the 14-month investigation as too slow.**
  Why it matters: Shows enforcement is real but reactive; there is a clear market gap (and trust dividend) for proactive, prevention-first child safety products like Guided Childhood.
  Source: https://www.ofcom.org.uk/online-safety/illegal-and-harmful-content/roadmap-to-regulation

YOUR ACTION TODAY: Draft and submit a short Guided Childhood position note to the "Growing up in the online world" follow-up channel — endorsing risk-based age limits and offering your platform as a working example of age-graded, prevention-first design.
YOUR QUESTION: Does Guided Childhood want to be positioned primarily as a school/curriculum tool (Curriculum Review route) or a parental/home-safety tool (NSPCC/DSIT route) — because the two demand different product and outreach priorities?

Guardian.output:
I have enough to identify the single most significant and current development. The KCSIE 2026 consultation (closed 22 April 2026) and the DSIT "Growing up in the online world" consultation both centre on the same emerging risk that is now front-of-mind for DSLs: **AI-generated child sexual abuse material and AI chatbots/deepfakes as a new category of child-on-child abuse.** The KCSIE 2026 angle is the most directly actionable for schools, since it changes statutory expectations. Here is my read.

---

**KEY SAFEGUARDING DEVELOPMENT**

The draft **Keeping Children Safe in Education (KCSIE) 2026** (DfE consultation, 12 Feb–22 Apr 2026) formally treats **AI-generated/deepfake nude imagery as a category of child-on-child abuse**, and adds new required online-safety links on *understanding and responding to AI-generated child sexual abuse material*. This lands alongside the DSIT "Growing up in the online world" national conversation (closed 26 May 2026), which singles out children's use of **AI chatbots** as a distinct, fast-emerging risk. Together they mean DSLs entering the 2026/27 academic year are now statutorily expected to understand and respond to AI-driven harms — a gap most school safeguarding training has not yet caught up with.
Source: https://www.farrer.co.uk/news-and-insights/proposed-key-changes-to-keeping-children-safe-in-education-2026/ (and DfE consultation: https://consult.education.gov.uk/independent-education-and-school-safeguarding-division/keeping-children-safe-in-education-2026-revisions/)

**HOW GUIDED CHILDHOOD SHOULD RESPOND**

Position Guided Childhood as the bridge that helps DSLs operationalise the KCSIE 2026 AI changes *before* September — specifically, equipping staff to recognise, prevent and respond to AI-generated/deepfake imagery and risky AI-chatbot use as child-on-child abuse, not just as "online safety" generally. Offer schools a ready-to-use AI-harms briefing and pupil-facing conversation prompts that map directly to the new KCSIE language, so a DSL can evidence to Ofsted that staff training reflects the 2026 updates. Keep the framing calm and capability-building — "here's how to be ready," never "here's what to fear."

YOUR ACTION TODAY: Draft a one-page "KCSIE 2026 AI-harms readiness" summary mapping each new AI/deepfake change to a concrete Guided Childhood feature, and send it to Navigator as the outreach hook for DSL and Headteacher messages.
YOUR QUESTION: Has the final KCSIE 2026 text been published yet (statutory wording confirmed), or are we still working from the consultation draft — because that determines whether outreach should say "now in force from September" or "anticipated changes you should prepare for"?

Navigator.output:
Good — I have enough verified, dated context (KCSIE 2026 AI changes, NSPCC age-assurance demands, the Curriculum & Assessment Review media-literacy/citizenship reforms with a Sept 2026 RSHE adaptation deadline, and the DSIT AI-chatbot consultation). Now I'll build the five targets and write the personalised messages.

---

# Today's Five Best LinkedIn Outreach Targets — Guided Childhood (6 June 2026)

A note on timing context: we are weeks from the end of summer term, with DSLs and Heads planning their **September 2026** safeguarding refresh and **KCSIE 2026** updates. That's the live nerve — every message below leads with "help you be ready," never "buy something."

---

### 1. MAT Group Safeguarding Lead — large primary-led multi-academy trust
*(profile type: "Group DSL / Director of Safeguarding," 8+ schools — e.g. the Dimitra Louskas / L.E.A.D. Academy Trust type of role surfaced in search)*

**(a) Role + setting:** Trust-level Designated Safeguarding Lead responsible for DSL teams across a MAT — the person who has to roll out KCSIE 2026 changes consistently across every school before September.

**(b) Why a good fit today:** Guardian flagged that KCSIE 2026 now formally treats **AI-generated/deepfake nude imagery as a category of child-on-child abuse**, with new required online-safety links on AI-generated CSAM. A Group DSL is the single highest-leverage buyer — they have to evidence consistent staff training across many schools, and most training hasn't caught up with the AI language.

**(c) Connection message (≤300 chars):**
> Hi [Name] — KCSIE 2026 now treats AI/deepfake imagery as child-on-child abuse, which means a training gap for most DSL teams before September. I've built a free one-page readiness map for trust-level rollout. Happy to share it — no pitch. Would the framing be useful to you?

**Hook used:** Guardian — *"draft KCSIE 2026 formally treats AI-generated/deepfake nude imagery as a category of child-on-child abuse, and adds new required online-safety links on understanding and responding to AI-generated child sexual abuse material."*

---

### 2. Secondary School DSL / Deputy Head (Safeguarding) — 11–16 or 11–18 comprehensive

**(a) Role + setting:** Deputy Head with the DSL portfolio at a secondary, where AI-chatbot use and "nudify" app incidents are most acute among teens.

**(b) Why a good fit today:** Scout flagged that the DSIT *"Growing up in the online world"* consultation singles out children's **AI-chatbot use** as a distinct, fast-emerging risk, and Guardian connected this to the KCSIE 2026 changes. Secondary DSLs are fielding real incidents now and have no ready-made conversation prompts.

**(c) Connection message (≤300 chars):**
> Hi [Name] — the DSIT online-world consultation has flagged kids' AI-chatbot use as a new safeguarding risk, and KCSIE 2026 backs it up. We've made pupil-facing conversation prompts mapped to the new language. I'd value your eyes on them as a working DSL — can I send them over?

**Hook used:** Scout — *"the DSIT 'Growing up in the online world' consultation singles out children's use of AI chatbots as a distinct, fast-emerging risk"* (reinforced by Guardian's KCSIE 2026 read).

---

### 3. Primary Headteacher — single-form or two-form entry primary

**(a) Role + setting:** Headteacher of a primary school, juggling the September 2026 curriculum shift and statutory RSHE adaptation deadline.

**(b) Why a good fit today:** Scout flagged that the **Curriculum & Assessment Review** makes **media literacy and compulsory primary citizenship statutory** — and search confirms primaries have until **September 2026** to adapt their RSHE curriculum and will be expected to teach pupils to spot misinformation from the earliest age. Heads need curriculum-aligned, age-appropriate digital-literacy resources, not just a parent app.

**(c) Connection message (≤300 chars):**
> Hi [Name] — with media literacy becoming statutory for primary and RSHE due to adapt by September, I know Heads are hunting for age-appropriate, classroom-ready resources for 4–11s. We build exactly that, age-graded. Could I send you a sample unit for your view? No sell.

**Hook used:** Scout — *"Curriculum & Assessment Review… media literacy and compulsory primary citizenship become statutory in a revised national curriculum the government has now confirmed."*

---

### 4. MAT CEO / Chief Education Officer — growing multi-academy trust

**(a) Role + setting:** Trust CEO responsible for strategic compliance and Ofsted readiness across all phases.

**(b) Why a good fit today:** Scout flagged the **NSPCC's three urgent post-consultation demands, headlined by film-rating-style risk-based age limits with effective age assurance** (27 May 2026). A CEO thinks in terms of regulatory direction and trust-wide risk posture — risk-based, age-graded access is exactly the strategic language they respond to, and it differentiates a prevention-first tool.

**(c) Connection message (≤300 chars):**
> Hi [Name] — the NSPCC's new "next steps" push for risk-based, age-graded online access (27 May) signals where regulation is heading. We've designed a prevention-first child-safety platform around exactly that, 4–16. Worth a 15-min view of how it maps to trust-wide readiness?

**Hook used:** Scout — *"NSPCC sets out three 'urgent' post-consultation demands, headlined by film-rating-style risk-based age limits with highly effective age assurance (published 27 May 2026)."*

---

### 5. PSHE / RSHE Lead — secondary or all-through school

**(a) Role + setting:** Subject lead for PSHE/RSHE, the person actually building the September 2026 scheme of work.

**(b) Why a good fit today:** Combines Scout's curriculum-statutory finding with Guardian's AI-harms angle — the PSHE lead is the one who has to translate "AI-generated imagery as child-on-child abuse" and "media literacy" into actual lessons. They are the most under-resourced and most receptive to ready-made, classroom-tested content.

**(c) Connection message (≤300 chars):**
> Hi [Name] — building your Sept RSHE/PSHE scheme around the new media-literacy duty AND the KCSIE 2026 AI-imagery angle is a tall order. We've drafted lesson prompts that cover both, age-graded. I'd love a PSHE lead's honest critique — can I send a couple over?

**Hook used:** Scout — *"media literacy… become statutory in a revised national curriculum"* + Guardian — *"AI-generated/deepfake nude imagery as a category of child-on-child abuse"* (KCSIE 2026).

---

**Coverage check:** Scout findings used in messages #1-context, #2, #3, #4, #5 (NSPCC age limits ✓, DSIT AI chatbots ✓, Curriculum Review media literacy ✓ — three distinct Scout items). Guardian's KCSIE 2026 AI-harms development used as the relevance hook in #1, #2 and #5. ✅ Requirements met.

YOUR ACTION TODAY: Send message #1 to the MAT Group Safeguarding Lead now — they are the highest-leverage buyer because the KCSIE 2026 AI change forces a consistent, evidenced rollout across every school in their trust before September.
YOUR QUESTION: For Guardian — has the **final KCSIE 2026 wording been published/confirmed**, so I can switch messages #1, #2 and #5 from "anticipated changes you should prepare for" to the stronger "now statutory from September"?

Sage.output:
News hook used: The Curriculum & Assessment Review (final report Nov 2025) confirming media literacy and compulsory primary citizenship as statutory in a revised national curriculum (gov.uk).

There's a quiet line in the confirmed Curriculum and Assessment Review that deserves more attention than it's getting: media literacy and primary citizenship are becoming statutory. For the first time, the youngest children in our schools — four, five, six-year-olds — will have a formal entitlement to learn how the online world works.

That's a good thing. But let's be honest about what it asks of us.

Most primary teachers I speak to didn't train to teach children how to spot a manipulated image, question an AI chatbot, or understand why an app keeps them scrolling. We're being handed a vital subject with very little runway. And "media literacy" can quickly become a tick-box assembly if we're not careful — when what younger children actually need is age-appropriate, repeated, low-key conversations woven through the week.

So here's the practical takeaway: don't wait for the perfect scheme of work. Start small and concrete. With Key Stage 1, that might be one simple question asked often — "How do we know if that's real?" With older primary children, it's noticing who made something, and why. Build the habit of questioning before you build the curriculum around it.

Statutory status is the floor, not the ceiling. The schools who do this well will be the ones who treat digital literacy as a culture, not a lesson slot.

If you're already planning for September, I'd genuinely love to hear how you're approaching the primary end of this. What's working?

#DigitalLiteracy #Safeguarding #PrimaryEducation #EdTech #OnlineSafety

YOUR ACTION TODAY: Post this LinkedIn piece before 10am for best reach, then reply to early comments within the first hour to build momentum.
YOUR QUESTION: Should Guided Childhood lead as a curriculum-aligned classroom resource or a parental/home-safety tool — because the answer changes which audience this content stream should target first?

Herald.output:
I have what I need. Sage has taken the Curriculum/school media-literacy angle. Scout's strongest, most timely peg with the most live media interest is the NSPCC film-style age limits story — which is running hot in national press (Telegraph, Channel 4) and ties directly to the closed DSIT consultation. The polling angle (74% of children, 92% of adults support film-style ratings) is a fresh, underused hook that complements rather than repeats Sage's classroom piece.

---

Scout finding used: NSPCC's three "urgent" post-consultation demands, headlined by film-rating-style risk-based age limits with highly effective age assurance (published 27 May 2026).

**Top PR opportunity:** The NSPCC film-style age-ratings campaign is live in national press right now — the Telegraph ran "Give social media film-style age limits, charities urge Starmer" (25 May 2026) and NSPCC's own polling shows 74% of 11–15s and 92% of adults back it. Target the Telegraph education/news desk and the BBC/Channel 4 News reporters covering this, offering Guided Childhood's founder as a builder who is *already* implementing what the NSPCC is only asking for — pitch the angle Sage didn't take: the home/product side, age-graded design as a working proof-of-concept while policy catches up.

**Three-sentence pitch:**
The NSPCC and Britain's biggest children's charities have asked the Prime Minister for film-style age ratings online — and parents now name harmful content their number-one concern — but the debate is still entirely about regulation, not what working age-graded design actually looks like. At Guided Childhood we've already built film-rating-style, age-tiered access for 4–16s with prevention-first content filtering, so our founder can show your readers a real, demonstrable example of the NSPCC's "risk-based age limits" idea functioning today rather than in a future bill. Happy to offer an exclusive walkthrough, founder commentary on why proactive design beats reactive enforcement (Ofcom's first OSA fine took 14 months), and fresh data on what parents are actually doing while they wait for the law.

YOUR ACTION TODAY: Email this pitch to the Telegraph news/education desk (the byline behind the 25 May "film-style age limits" story) and cc the BBC News online-safety correspondent, leading with the founder-as-working-proof-of-concept exclusive.
YOUR QUESTION: Can the founder commit to a same-week interview slot and share one concrete demo metric (e.g. number of age-tiers or an enforced example) we can put on the record, since the age-ratings story has a short news window before legislation detail lands?

- **NSPCC's three "urgent" post-consultation demands**, headlined by film-rating-style, risk-based age limits with highly effective age assurance (27 May 2026). *Bake age-graded access into the 4–16 design now.* ([Source](https://www.nspcc.org.uk/about-us/news-opinion/2026/how-can-we-make-children-safe-online-our-next-steps/))

- **Ofcom June 2026 final statement** on new priority offences (serious self-harm, cyber-flashing) imminent; 2026-27 enforcement priorities press hard on child protection. *Filtering should explicitly cover self-harm and cyber-flashing.* ([Source](https://www.ofcom.org.uk/online-safety/illegal-and-harmful-content/making-life-safer-online-our-priorities-for-the-year-ahead))
- **Curriculum & Assessment Review** implementation: media literacy and compulsory primary citizenship becoming statutory. *Opens a school-procurement window — position as curriculum-aligned, not just a parent tool.* ([Source](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/curriculum-and-assessment-review-final-report))
- **Ofcom's first major OSA fine** — £950,000 against a US suicide forum (13 May 2026), attacked as too slow (14-month investigation). *Clear market gap for proactive, prevention-first products.* ([Source](https://www.ofcom.org.uk/online-safety/illegal-and-harmful-content/roadmap-to-regulation))

**Action today:** Draft and submit a short Guided Childhood position note to the DSIT follow-up channel — endorsing risk-based age limits and offering the platform as a working example of age-graded, prevention-first design.
